sop.coo_evaluation:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaints & incident logging** – no documented process for tracking complaints (service, safety, conduct), escalation triggers, or resolution timelines. Critical for duty of care & pattern-spotting.
- **Health & safety incident response** – no SOP for injury on-site, near-misses, equipment failure, or safeguarding concerns (especially relevant in women-only space). Who documents? Who notifies? Retention of records?
- **Lapsed member win-back campaign** – onboarding is SOPed but no systematic re-engagement sequence for members who've gone inactive (triggers, messaging cadence, owner).
- **Coach performance management & feedback cycles** – no documented process for 1:1s, performance review frequency, corrective action steps, or termination protocols.
- **Financial controls & reconciliation** – no SOPs for cash handling, payment disputes, refund requests, or monthly P&L review.
- **Facility deep-clean & equipment maintenance schedule** – you have daily/weekly cleaning, but no preventive maintenance SOP (bar/dumbbell inspection, flooring checks, air quality, pest control audit).
- **New product/class launch process** – who decides? How do you pilot? Market testing SOP? Member comms sequence?
- **Staffing crisis protocols** – no documented plan for sudden coach/staff absence, cover scheduling, or temp staffing triggers.

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Client onboarding** – likely missing: first-class safety briefing checklist, fitness assessment documentation, liability waiver capture method, and follow-up sequence at Day 3/7/14. Verify it covers all intake questions (injuries, medications, goals).
- **Coach daily/weekly SOPs** – check if they cover: class set-up/teardown time (how early/late?), music/

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ACTION PLAN

## REVENUE — BIGGEST PRIORITY

**AR/M (Average Revenue per Member) is collapsing.** June sits at £120.48, down from May's £165.56 (27% drop) and well below the Sept–Dec average of £151.51. With 147 members, you're leaving ~£6,700/month on the table vs. your peak pricing power. The bigger studio should have *increased* AR/M through premium class tiers or ancillary sales, not crushed it.

- **Audit membership mix immediately.** You likely onboarded budget-tier members post-relocation to fill the new space. Segment June's 147 by plan type (standard/premium/class pack). Introduce a "Premium+" tier (£5–8/month uplift) tied to peak-time access or unlimited guest passes; convert 20–30 of the new cohort by month-end (+£1,200–2,400/month).

- **Launch PT/nutrition add-ons.** June non-people expenses of £9,390 suggest facility/utilities are now controllable; you have breathing room. Use it: create a tiered personal training menu (3 sessions/month at £45–60) or partner with a local nutritionist (20% revenue share, zero fixed cost). Target 8–12 uptakes by end of July (+£1,800–2,880/month recurring).

- **Churn is unacceptable.** June is 0% (good), but May (5.74%), April (8.33%), and March (6.60%) show post-relocation turbulence. You've stabilized at 147 members, but lost momentum. Implement automated 30-day win-back emails (free class + 10% off renewal) for lapsed members and a "refer a friend, both get 1 month free" scheme. Targeting 6–8 reactivations/month = +£1,000/month.

## EXPENSES — QUICK WINS

- **Non-people costs spiked in Jan–Apr (£8.2k–£15.6k) and remain elevated (£9.4k in June).** Post-relocation stabilization should see £5k–6k/month. Itemize June's £9,390: if utilities/rent/insurance now run £6k, that's acceptable. If there's a one-off services bill or supplier overage, negotiate/terminate. Target £6,500/month by August (save £3k/month).

- **Payroll is reasonable but creeping.** June

staff.advice:
• **JoJo is significantly overloaded at 133 sessions.** She's carrying 56% of the coaching load and is the sole or primary coach for Personal Training (44 sessions) and Circuits (6 sessions). Redistribute some 1:1s to Grace and Eilis to free JoJo for class delivery and reduce burnout risk.

• **Grace is underutilised at 52 sessions and should absorb more Personal Training and Body Composition Scans.** She has capacity to take on consistent 1:1 slots beyond Mondays—especially given her founder role likely includes flexible scheduling. This would directly ease JoJo's burden.

• **Eilis has room to grow into 1:1 work (currently 11 PTs and 11 scans).** Gradually increase her Personal Training and scan load to build her confidence and skill, creating a second reliable stream for these revenue-generating services.

• **Circuits (6 sessions) relies entirely on JoJo.** Assign this class to Grace or Eilis on rotation so JoJo isn't the single point of failure and the class isn't dependent on one coach's availability.

• **Build: Upper is JoJo-heavy (15 of her sessions).** Distribute this class across the roster—Grace already teaches it (9 sessions), so use Eilis or reallocate to create a balanced rota and improve member choice.

• **Body Composition Scans are fragmented across all three coaches but demand peaks with JoJo.** Standardise the process and train all three to deliver them reliably so no single coach becomes a bottleneck for this revenue-generating service.

class_stats.suggestion:
**1. Consolidate low-attendance classes.** Engine, Capacity, Barbelle, Strongevity and Games Night are consistently underperforming (12-52 attendees per 90 days). Consider removing or merging these into the Build and Elevate classes your members clearly prefer, freeing up studio time for more popular offerings.

**2. Stack Build classes on Wednesday/Tuesday.** Your peak days (Wednesday 552, Tuesday 469) are when members show up most. Add extra Build: Upper and Build: Lower sessions during these days to capture demand, since these are your top performers in the last 30 days.

**3. Boost Monday and Saturday with targeted scheduling.** Monday (361) and Saturday (203) are your quietest. Schedule one popular class (Build or Elevate) at convenient times on these days—perhaps a Saturday morning Build class and a Monday lunchtime option—to drive weekend and weak-day attendance.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Build — Execution Playbook for Grace

• **Start with your revenue reality, not your wishes.** Pull last 3 months of transaction data — what are members actually *buying*? (Classes vs. PT vs. packages.) Build the menu around what's selling, then add 1-2 strategic gaps. Ignore services you *think* should exist.

• **Create two separate documents, not one.** Coach version = cost-to-deliver, margins, upsell triggers. Member version = benefits-first language (e.g., "6-week strength foundation" not "12 sessions"). Never let coaches see member pricing psychology notes.

• **Anchor pricing to local comp gyms, then price 15-20% higher with justification visible.** For The Forge specifically: "Women-only environment" and "Belfast's only [your strongest differentiator]" justify premium. List this *on* the member menu — it's your permission structure.

• **Test-launch with your most vocal 5-10 members first.** Show them draft menu in Week 1, collect friction points by Week 2, iterate by Week 3. They'll flag confusing tier-naming or missing options before you print/go live. Build hype while testing.

• **Lock in a 30-day "menu freeze" launch window.** Announce it's coming, let existing members pre-commit to packages (captures revenue surge + demand signal), then go live. Nothing kills momentum like constantly tweaking pricing after launch.

**Timeline: 2 weeks to draft, 1 week member feedback, live in Week 4.**

What's your current service offering look like?

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refine - Execution Playbook for Grace

**1. Pull your actual drop-off data FIRST**
Don't redesign from assumptions. Check Google Analytics for where visitors leave (homepage → pricing? About → contact?). Check your CRM for the most-asked questions from trial sign-ups. This tells you *which* page to fix and *what* confusion exists. Start there, not with a full redesign.

**2. Create a "clarity audit" using real member language**
Send 5-10 current members a quick message: "What confused you about us before you joined?" and "What do prospects always ask you about The Forge?" Use *their exact words* in your landing pages. Women's-only fitness has specific value props (safety, no male gaze, community) — make sure you're naming these directly, not vaguely.

**3. Reverse-engineer competitor pages + steal their FAQ structure**
Look at 3-4 competing women's gyms or local Belfast fitness spots. Grab their FAQ sections — you'll spot the universal pain points (pricing tiers, class times, beginner-friendly?, contracts, changing facilities). Create your own FAQ answering the same 8-10 questions with *your* angle.

**4. A/B test ONE element immediately**
Pick one landing page. Write two versions of your headline/hero section (one emphasizes community, one emphasizes results). Run them to your email list or use Google Optimize. You'll learn if clarity drives conversions. Deploy the winner in 2 weeks.

**5. Add a "Frequently Asked" section above the fold**
People scroll past walls of text. Put 3-4 biggest questions *before* they

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Internal Feedback System — Execution Play for The Forge

• **Start with post-class 60-second QR code surveys** — Don't wait for monthly forms; capture feedback when members are already there and engaged. One question per week rotated (class quality, facility cleanliness, instructor energy, scheduling needs). Takes 10 seconds to complete. Post the QR at exit with a small incentive (10 monthly responses = free class).

• **Make it visible/actionable immediately** — Print a one-page summary of last month's feedback and post it in the changing rooms with your *specific responses* ("You said crowded 6pm slots — we're adding a 6:15pm class"). Nothing kills engagement faster than feedback vanishing into a black hole. Members need to see their voice moved something.

• **Assign one staff member 30 mins/month to analyse & report** — Not a big project. One person reviews responses, spots patterns (facility complaint vs. one-off), flags anything urgent (class cancellation requests, injury concerns), and feeds into your quarterly planning. Keeps it lightweight but disciplined.

• **Layer in informal 1:1 chats during peak hours** — Your instructors/staff should ask 2-3 members per shift casually: "What would make your experience here even better?" Captures stuff people won't write down. Log it in a simple shared note.

• **Use feedback directly in your marketing** — Once you've got patterns, testimonials, and unmet needs, you have gold for your next campaign or service adjustment (e.g., "Members asked for X — we built it").

**Deadline:** QR code system live in 1 week. First monthly summary printed in 4

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Strategy for The Forge

**1. Start with your revenue-ranked service first**
Don't write all five at once. Identify which service generates the most recurring revenue (likely membership or PT) and nail that SOP in the next 3 days. This creates momentum and gives you a tested template. Once one is done, the others follow the same structure faster.

**2. Use your actual member journey as the skeleton**
Don't invent processes from a desk. Shadow 2-3 members through each service this week. Document what actually happens: payment, onboarding, check-ins, questions asked, drop-off points. Your SOP should reflect reality, not theory. This takes 4-5 hours but prevents writing useless docs.

**3. Build each SOP as a *member-facing* checklist first**
Write what the member needs to know/do (a simple 1-page workflow), then build the staff version behind it. This forces clarity—if you can't explain it simply to a member, your process is broken. Format as numbered steps with exact timings (e.g., "Day 1: email sent within 2 hours of signup").

**4. Assign one person as owner per service**
Give one team member responsibility for writing and owning each SOP. This prevents bottlenecking on you and creates accountability. Supply them with: (a) a template with headings, (b) 2-3 member examples to reference, (c) a Friday deadline.

**5. Test before locking it down**
After writing, have someone NOT involved follow the SOP with a real member. They'll catch gaps. Revise, then version-lock it—date it and

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Content Calendar for The Forge

• **Use a templated batching system, not daily posting:** Block out one 90-min session monthly (first Monday). Create all content for 4 weeks at once using a simple Google Sheet with columns: Date | Post Type | Hook | CTA | Graphic/Video Brief. This removes decision fatigue and prevents the "forgot to post" trap.

• **Anchor posts to your actual gym reality:** Don't create generic fitness content. Instead, tie posts to what's *actually happening*: member transformations (with permission), workout classes that week, "caught on camera" moments from the gym floor, member spotlights. This feels authentic and naturally encourages sharing.

• **Assign ONE person (probably you initially) as the approver:** Content sits in drafts until you review it. Takes 10 mins per week. Schedule everything via Buffer or Meta's native scheduler on Friday afternoons for the week ahead. Removes the "who's posting what" chaos.

• **Make 60% of posts *engagement bait, not broadcast*:** Instead of "New class schedule," ask "Which time slot works best for you?" or share a relatable women's gym struggle with a poll. This drives comments/shares—the algorithm's currency—and builds community, not just follower count.

• **Track the *one metric that matters*: new member inquiries from social.** Add a UTM code to your Linktree/website link ("utm_source=instagram"). Weekly check: How many people clicked through? Adjust post types based on what actually converts, not vanity metrics.

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Execute: Regular Videography Sessions with Jordan

**Lock in a recurring calendar invite NOW** — don't plan to "schedule later." Pick your 8-week dates for the next 12 months and send them to Jordan today. This removes friction and ensures it actually happens.

**Define what "done" looks like before the first shoot** — brief Jordan on 2-3 specific content gaps you need filled (e.g., "member transformation clips," "class highlight reels," "behind-the-scenes of the gym floor"). Vague shoots waste his time and yours.

**Batch your filming into half-day blocks** — get multiple pieces of content in one session rather than spreading shoots thin. This maximizes Jordan's efficiency, cuts costs, and gives you a content buffer for your socials (post 2-3 weeks out).

**Assign one person on your team as the "content liaison"** — they brief Jordan, manage member sign-offs/releases, and handle basic editing notes. Saves you from being the bottleneck; keeps momentum going.

**Build a simple swipe file immediately** — after each shoot, capture which videos actually drove member inquiries or engagement. Use this to brief Jordan for session #2. You're not guessing anymore; you're optimizing.

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemize Automations for 6-Week Trial Enquiries — Action Plan

• **Map the current broken path first (30 mins)**: Track one trial enquiry end-to-end right now. Where do leads sit? Email, WhatsApp, form responses? Identify the exact delay points before building automation — most gyms automate the wrong steps because they've never mapped reality.

• **Set up a 2-email sequence (not more)**: Day 1 (within 2 hours of enquiry) = welcome + trial booking link + "What's your main fitness goal?" Day 21 = "How's your trial going? Ready to join?" Use Zapier or Make.com to trigger these from your form/booking system. Test with 5 real leads before rolling out.

• **Automate the *friction kill*, not just the touchpoint**: The real bottleneck is probably follow-up gaps. Create a simple spreadsheet-to-SMS trigger: if a trial hasn't been booked within 24 hours, send one text ("Hi [name], let's get you started — pick a class here: [link]"). This takes 15 minutes to set up with Zapier.

• **Measure the one metric that matters**: Compare conversion rate on trials that get auto-sequences vs. manual follow-up for 6 weeks. Track: enquiries → bookings → member sign-ups. If automations lift conversion by 15%+, double down. If not, the problem isn't the system—it's offer/messaging.

• **Don't over-engineer for this trial period**: Use free tier tools (Zapier free, Google Forms, your existing email). Complexity kills execution. Once you

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Weekly Role Checklists + KPIs for The Forge Team

**1. Structure it backwards from revenue impact**
Don't list tasks — list the *measurable outcomes* each person drives weekly, then work backward to actions. Example: JoJo owns "£X in PT bookings" (her KPI), so her checklist includes: follow-up calls to lapsed clients, new inquiry responses within 2 hours, one referral request. This forces clarity on what actually matters.

**2. Make KPIs brutally specific to their role**
- **JoJo (assume PT/sales):** £[target] PT revenue, [#] new client inquiries converted, [#] referrals generated
- **Eilis (assume ops/retention):** member churn rate %, class attendance %, [#] member touch-points completed
- **Sarah (assume marketing/community):** [#] new leads, social engagement rate, event attendance numbers

Get Grace to agree these numbers *first* — then build checklists around hitting them.

**3. Lock it into a one-page format per person**
A4 sheet max. Top half: 3-4 KPIs + weekly targets. Bottom half: 6-8 specific actions (time-boxed, where possible). Laminate it. They carry it. Review it every Monday morning for 10 minutes.

**4. Embed peer accountability, not just boss monitoring**
Weekly 15-min standup: each person calls out their KPIs vs. actuals. This kills the "Grace's checklist" feeling — becomes *their* checklist. They own the gap.

**What does Grace need from you to lock

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Execute Your Team Vision Session – Concrete Steps

• **Pre-session: Send the SSO + 90-day plan 48 hours early** – not in the meeting. Your team needs to absorb it privately first, spot confusion, come ready to discuss their role (not learn basics in real time). Attach a one-pager: "Here's where we're going. Your job in this session: identify what blocks *you* from owning your lane."

• **Structure the 60-min session in three tight blocks** – (1) You talk SSO + 90-day numbers for 10 mins max (they've read it), (2) each team member reads their "lane" aloud for 2 mins (their KPIs, decisions they own, what success looks like for them specifically), (3) last 30 mins: "What resources, clarity, or permission do you need from me THIS MONTH?" Write every answer down. This flips the vibe from "listen to the plan" to "how do we unblock you."

• **Define "lane" explicitly with role-specific examples** – Don't say "sales lane." Say: "Sarah owns: member sign-ups (target 8/month), retention calls (weekly check-ins on inactive members), referral incentive management. You make decisions on messaging and timing. I approve budget only." Vague lanes = unclear ownership = slow execution.

• **Close with one commitment per person they state out loud** – Not a to-do list you hand them. Ask: "What's one thing you're committing to by [specific date] that moves the 90-day needle?" They own it verbally. You note it. Follow up individually within 3 days with

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalance for The Forge

• **Map actual hours NOW, not job descriptions.** Have each team member (coaches, reception, management) track their real time allocation for one week—what they're *actually* doing vs. what you think they're doing. You'll spot where someone's covering three roles while another has slack. This beats a spreadsheet conversation every time.

• **Identify the bottleneck person first.** Usually one staff member is absorbing overflow. Ask yourself: *Who do people come to when something breaks?* That's your over-stretched person. Pull them out of reactive tasks immediately by documenting their hidden responsibilities so someone else can own them.

• **Create a "low-value task dump" list.** What's currently being done that doesn't drive member retention, revenue, or experience? (Admin tasks, repetitive emails, low-priority cleaning.) Automate 2-3 of these this week (scheduling software, email templates, checklists for part-timers). This frees capacity without hiring.

• **Rebalance around *your revenue drivers*, not fairness.** Your coaches who run packed classes or 1-on-1 PTs shouldn't be handling member complaints or stock ordering. Shift non-revenue tasks to lower-billing-hour staff or create rotating admin days. Protect your money-makers' focus.

• **Lock in the changes with weekly 15-min check-ins for 4 weeks.** Don't audit and disappear. Monthly reviews let problems rebuild. Weekly touch-bases catch "we're drowning again" early and show you're serious about the rebalance sticking.

What's your biggest pain point right now—who's clearly stretched?

# Cost Reduction Playbook for The Forge

• **Audit staffing first** — Map actual floor coverage needs vs. current rota. You likely have peak hours (6-9am, 5-7pm) heavily staffed while midday crawls. Shift to part-time/casual staff for off-peak. This typically cuts 15-20% of payroll without service loss. Start with one week's actual foot traffic data.

• **Renegotiate supplier contracts NOW** — Equipment maintenance, cleaning supplies, utilities. Get 3 competing quotes and tell your current providers you're shopping around. Most will match or beat 8-12% just to keep the business. Takes 2 hours of calls, 5-10% savings realistically.

• **Trim the membership perks that don't convert** — Are you offering included PT sessions, merchandise, or classes nobody uses? Kill those. Cut dead weight from your offering and reallocate that budget to what actually drives retention (the two things your members actually want).

• **Energy audit your facility** — LED lighting conversion, thermostat zones in unused areas, shower/equipment runtime schedules. Gyms are energy-heavy; 10-15% utility cuts are achievable. One contractor walkthrough costs £200-300 but identifies quick wins.

• **Review your software stack** — Membership management, booking, billing. You're probably paying for features you don't use. Switching platforms or trimming subscriptions (Spotify in the gym? Cut it) often yields 8-12% in admin/tech savings.

**Action: Start with payroll audit this week. That's where 6-7% of your 10% lives.**

# Monthly Finance KPI Update — Execution Plan

• **Set a fixed day (e.g., 3rd of month) and block 90 mins non-negotiable.** Use a simple Google Sheet with: member revenue, churn rate, class utilization %, average spend per member, operating costs as % of revenue. This becomes your single source of truth — no hunting through emails.

• **Pull data in this order to save time:** Bank transactions (5 mins) → membership software export (5 mins) → manual PT/class add-ons (10 mins). Do it once weekly in 20 mins rather than cramming monthly — easier to spot issues early.

• **Create a one-page "month snapshot" — literally 6 KPIs max.** Trend arrows (↑↓) vs last month. Share with yourself, not a 20-slide report. Grace needs to glance and know: "Am I on track for Q targets?"

• **Set one decision threshold per metric.** Example: "If churn hits 8%, I call a staff meeting that week." If utilization drops below 65%, you're running a discount promo immediately. No KPI sits unacted-on.

• **Use the data to kill one thing monthly.** Classes underperforming? Member feedback weak? You'll see it in the numbers. Use this update to make one pivot before next month begins.

What's your current tracking system — spreadsheet, dedicated software, or scattered files?

# Financial Forecasting for The Forge — Execution Play

• **Start with 3 scenarios, not a 10-year plan.** Model: base case (current trajectory), growth case (+20% members in 12 months), and stress case (10% churn). Use actual monthly revenue from last 18 months + your known fixed costs (rent, staff, utilities). This takes 2-3 hours in a spreadsheet and gives you decision-making clarity instead of vanity projections.

• **Nail your unit economics first.** Calculate: average revenue per member/month, cost to acquire one member (marketing spend ÷ new signups), lifetime value (avg membership length × monthly revenue − acquisition cost). If LTV isn't 3x+ your acquisition cost, forecasting won't save a broken model—you need to fix pricing or retention *now*.

• **Build a cash runway tracker, not a P&L.** Women's fitness has seasonal dips (January peaks, summer slumps). Map out monthly: cash in (memberships, PT packages, merchandise), cash out (payroll, rent, equipment, marketing), and minimum cash buffer needed. This tells you if you can afford that new hire or marketing push *this quarter*.

• **Identify your 2-3 financial levers to test.** From forecasting, ask: what moves revenue most—class frequency, PT uptake, retention rate, price increase? Pick ONE to forecast impact on (e.g., "if we move from £49 to £59/month, what happens to churn and net revenue?"). Run the math. If it's positive, you've got your Q1 focus.

• **Review monthly, not quarterly.** Set a recurring

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Build Your Service P&L — Fast & Actionable

**1. Start with 3 columns, not a spreadsheet nightmare**
Create a simple Google Sheet with: Service | Total Monthly Revenue | Total Monthly Costs | Gross Margin %. Don't overthink it. PT costs = trainer wages + insurance allocation. Classes = instructor pay + music/equipment maintenance. Add-ons = COGS + minimal overhead. You'll spot the winners in 20 minutes.

**2. Assign costs ruthlessly (this is where most gym owners fail)**
PT is easy — direct trainer cost. For classes, divide your monthly studio rent + utilities by total class hours to get a hourly burden, then multiply by each class length. For add-ons (protein shakes, merch), use actual product costs. If you don't know a cost, estimate conservatively.

**3. Identify your actual profit drivers in week one**
Run this today. You'll probably discover one service (likely PT at premium rates or a specific class type) is carrying the gym while something else bleeds money. That's your action list for next month.

**4. Use this to kill the sacred cows**
If a low-margin service is taking up prime studio time or trainer capacity, you now have the data to cut it or raise its price 20% without guilt. That's the whole point — making emotional decisions evidence-based.

**5. Track it monthly, obsessively**
Update that same sheet every month. The margin will shift as you scale. Make it your single decision-making tool for the next 12 months.

What's your gut telling you is your most profitable service right now?

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Fix Your Automations: Tactical Breakdown

• **Start with your CRM/email platform audit TODAY** — Export all active automations (Mailchimp, HubSpot, Zapier, whatever you use). Look for duplicate triggers (e.g., "new enquiry" firing the same welcome email twice from different workflows). Kill duplicates immediately — they're eroding trust and wasting sends. Screenshot the kill list so you can explain it later.

• **Map your actual enquiry-to-trial journey in writing** — From someone landing on your website → submitting a form → receiving first email → booking trial → attending → converting to member. Walk through it yourself using a test email address. You'll find gaps (like no SMS reminder 24hrs before trial, or trial confirmation missing payment terms). Document exactly where automations should fire and what they should say.

• **Test the money-critical steps manually** — Specifically: does enquiry form submission trigger a booking link? Does the trial confirmation include gym location/parking/what to bring? Does the post-trial email go out automatically *only if they don't convert*? These three breaks kill conversions; test them this week with real team members playing the customer.

• **Assign one person as automation owner going forward** — Audits fail when no one owns them. Pick someone (even part-time) to review this quarterly and monitor open/click rates on each automation. If any email has <20% open rate, it's either poorly timed or poorly written — fix it.

• **Quick win:** Add a manual review step — Before automations go live, have someone read every email as if they're a prospect. Bad copy or broken logic ruins good infrastructure.

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# 3 AI Automations for The Forge (Concrete Actions)

**Task 1: Member Onboarding Emails**
- Use Zapier + ChatGPT to auto-generate personalized welcome sequences triggered when someone books a trial. Feed in: member name, fitness goals (from intake form), start date. Output: 5-email sequence that feels personal but runs on autopilot. Saves ~4 hours/week of manual writing. Cost: ~€30/month for Zapier.

**Task 2: Class Waitlist Management**
- Set up a simple Google Form → Zapier → Gmail automation that bumps waitlist members to "confirmed" status and sends them a pre-written confirmation + gym access code when spots open. No more manual emails chasing cancellations. Frees you up during peak cancellation windows (Tuesday mornings, Fridays).

**Task 3: Member Feedback/Review Requests**
- Build a Typeform survey that auto-triggers 48 hours after a member completes 5 classes (track via your booking system). ChatGPT writes 3 different versions so it doesn't feel robotic. Route 5-star reviews → Google/Instagram auto-post reminders; critical feedback → your Slack. Builds social proof passively while capturing real product intelligence.

**Start here:** Pick whichever task wastes YOUR time most actively (not just admin noise). Build that one first. You'll see ROI fast and the confidence to scale the others.

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity Execution for The Forge Website

**Right now, today:**

• **Get an SSL certificate (HTTPS) immediately** — if your site isn't already HTTPS, you're leaking trust and Google's penalizing you. Takes 15 mins through your hosting provider (usually £10-20/year). Non-negotiable before asking for card payments or emails.

• **Audit what data you're actually collecting** — member signups, payment info, email lists. Write down exactly what you're storing. This defines your real security risk. Most gyms collect way more than they need (cut it).

• **Install Wordfence (if WordPress) or equivalent firewall** — handles 95% of attacks without you doing anything. 30 mins setup. Focus here before anything fancy.

**This week:**

• **Set payment processing through Stripe/Square, NOT your own server** — never store card data yourself. This shifts liability away from you and is legally essential in UK. If you're doing this already, verify it's genuinely PCI-compliant (ask your payment processor directly).

• **Password audit + 2FA for admin accounts** — use 1Password or Bitwarden to force strong passwords on yourself, staff, any tools (email, hosting, social accounts). One compromised account tanks everything.

**The frame:** You're not building Fort Knox. You're closing the obvious holes that actual competitors/bad actors exploit. Done beats perfect.

What's your current setup — WordPress or another platform?

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

- Cost-per-lead has tripled in two months (£6 → £20) whilst lead volume has collapsed 70% (69 → 21), signalling either creative fatigue, audience saturation, or platform algorithm shift. June spend dropped but efficiency cratered—you're not getting a discount, you're getting worse inventory.
- Close rate has halved (29% in Apr → 48% in May → 48% in June *but* on far fewer leads), masking a deeper problem: you're now only converting higher-intent prospects because the ad is reaching fewer people period, not because messaging improved.
- Profit per lead has swung wildly (£22 in Apr down to £73 in Jun on raw math, but that's misleading)—the real story is June's 21 leads will likely yield only £10.4k revenue annualised at this trajectory, vs April's 69 leads would've hit £34k+. You're optimising for margin on a shrinking base.

**Actions**

1. **Pause and audit creative immediately.** April's ads are dead (or close). Pull the three highest-spending creatives from June and check their frequency capping and decay. Test 4–5 entirely new creative angles next week: lifestyle/community (women-only locker rooms, social events), before/after transformations (longer-form video), and objection-handling (price justification, no-experience welcome). Run these at 50% budget allocation to fresh lookalike + saved audiences.

2. **Narrow targeting ruthlessly for July.** Your broad audiences are exhausted. Layer in: women 25–45, Belfast postcodes only, engagement on previous campaigns, and exclude anyone who saw ads in May/June but didn't convert (they've decided). Test a separate small-budget (£40/week) campaign to cold traffic aged 18–24 or 45+ to find untapped segments; if either outperforms, scale.

3. **Shift bid strategy to *lead quality* not volume.** You're chasing leads at £20 because the algorithm is serving tire-kickers. Move from Lowest Cost to Target Cost at £12–15/lead (based on your 48% close rate =
